abstract: The landscape of international investment law is defined by a dense and intricate network of nearly 3,000 bilateral and regional agreements, supported by key procedural frameworks such as the ICSID and New York Conventions. This paper examines the historical evolution of investment regulation, tracing its journey from the colonial era and "gunboat diplomacy" to the post-colonial rise of the New International Economic Order and the subsequent proliferation of bilateral investment treaties (BITs) in the neoliberal era. It provides a comprehensive analysis of the core substantive protections afforded to investors—including safeguards against expropriation, the guarantee of fair and equitable treatment, and non-discrimination standards—while highlighting the interpretative challenges and systemic tensions that arise when balancing private property rights against a state’s sovereign right to regulate in the public interest. Furthermore, the paper scrutinizes the procedural architecture of investor-state dispute settlement (ISDS), addressing contentious phenomena such as treaty shopping, forum shopping, and the "revolving door" of arbitration professionals. By situating these legal rules within their historical and political contexts, the study illuminates the current shift toward re-politicization and reform in response to growing concerns over transparency, consistency, and the legitimacy of the global investment governance regime.
